nodes.label: Graphify
nodes.rationale: Builds a permanent map of an entire codebase and exports it as Obsidian markdown so an AI assistant never rereads the same files, walking a graph of relationships instead of loading raw files.

nodes.label: Token tax (AI forgetting cost)
nodes.rationale: Hidden cost of stateless AI assistants: rereading 200 files from scratch each new chat burns ~44,200 tokens asking the same question 7 different ways across a week.

nodes.label: Persistent codebase memory
nodes.rationale: Teach the AI the codebase once and it never forgets; Obsidian becomes a searchable, linkable, AI-readable record so the model never re-ingests files it already understood.

nodes.label: Tree-sitter one-pass parse
nodes.rationale: Reads the codebase exactly once, parsing every file with tree-sitter to extract relationships that matter: which functions call which, how services connect, what schemas feed which APIs.

nodes.label: Explicit relationship graph
nodes.rationale: Not just a pile of files but a map, e.g. user service connects to DB pool and sessions, which connects to auth.

nodes.label: Obsidian markdown export with wikilinks
nodes.rationale: Converts the graph into markdown notes (auth.md, sessions.md, db.md) each with wikilinks pointing to the others, forming a navigable second brain.

nodes.label: Graph-walk instead of file load
nodes.rationale: On a question the assistant walks three hops across the graph (user service -> DB pool -> sessions) rather than loading 50 files, claimed 71x more efficient.

nodes.label: Confidence-labeled relationships
nodes.rationale: Each relationship is tagged Extracted (came straight from code, trust it), Inferred (AI reasoned it, worth a second look), or Ambiguous (flagged for human verification) as a guardrail against hallucinations.

nodes.label: Hallucination guardrail via evidence check
nodes.rationale: Example: AI claims tokens rotate every 15 minutes; the linter asks 'found it in the code?' — no evidence, so the claim is flagged for removal.

nodes.label: Git-commit hook graph rebuild
nodes.rationale: Because code ships and schemas/APIs evolve, graphify rebuilds the graph on every git commit via a hook to keep the second brain in sync with reality.

nodes.label: Weekly lint pass
nodes.rationale: A critical maintenance step run once a week that checks the wiki for contradictions, dead pages, and fabricated claims; skipping it for weeks lets the second brain fill with stale claims the AI will confidently repeat.

nodes.label: Merge heterogeneous sources into one graph
nodes.rationale: SQL schemas, terraform, and architecture PDFs can be merged into one graph; new team members get an interactive map instead of a 200-slide onboarding deck.

nodes.label: Depends on clean codebase structure
nodes.rationale: Works best on codebases with clear structure and explicit relationships; circular dependencies and implicit conventions make the graph reflect that chaos — it is not a substitute for fixing bad architecture.

nodes.label: Graphify knowledge base
nodes.rationale: Graphify points at an existing workspace and turns it into a knowledge base of nodes and relationship edges, giving Claude Code a deeper, more contextual understanding of the workspace in minutes.

nodes.label: Graphify Claude Code plugin
nodes.rationale: Installed via the /plugin command in Claude Code; runs a skill that builds the knowledge base and works in any database/codebase.

nodes.label: Node and edge graph of concepts
nodes.rationale: Graphify relates concepts together as nodes connected by edges; even without full context this lets the AI know which concepts are related.

nodes.label: graphify-out folder
nodes.rationale: Graphify writes its output into a separate graphify-out folder, kept apart from workspace files as its own representation of the codebase.

nodes.label: graph.html visualization
nodes.rationale: A graph.html file in graphify-out renders the knowledge graph visually when opened in a browser.

nodes.label: graphify obsidian command
nodes.rationale: Converts the graphify knowledge base into an Obsidian vault of markdown notes (one note per node) because Obsidian uses markdown files rather than graphify's native format.

nodes.label: Obsidian vault (one note per node)
nodes.rationale: The graph becomes ~2,186 markdown notes, one per node, that Claude Code can be pointed at and route into for a contextual knowledge graph.

nodes.label: Relationships-without-context gap
nodes.rationale: Graphify's generated nodes relate concepts but do not carry the underlying source context; the nodes are valuable for relatedness yet weak for retrieval until the actual context is ported into them.

nodes.label: Reintegrating workspace context into nodes
nodes.rationale: A follow-up natural-language instruction tells Claude Code to duplicate the source context (transcripts, journals, articles, markdown) from the original workspace into the relevant Obsidian nodes, so each note holds context, not just relationships — making the vault powerful for retrieval.

nodes.label: Loop / goal don't-stop directive
nodes.rationale: To ensure completeness across ~2,186 files in one pass, the speaker uses an autonomous instruction like '/loop over' or 'goal: don't stop until every node has its relevant context and every markdown file's context is duplicated into the relevant nodes' — driving the agent to iterate over all files without stopping.

nodes.label: Claude Code routed into the knowledge graph vault
nodes.rationale: Starting a Claude Code session directly inside the graphify-obsidian vault lets it answer hyper-specific cross-concept questions by locating the exact nodes instead of grepping and reading every file.

nodes.label: Bash-command node lookup
nodes.rationale: Because the knowledge base is prebuilt, Claude Code answers a complex query by running only about three bash/grep commands against specific nodes and markdown files, finding the exact information in ~30 seconds.

nodes.label: Token-efficient prebuilt-graph retrieval
nodes.rationale: A prebuilt knowledge graph lets the agent search and find exactly what it needs with very few tokens — roughly a few hundred to at most a thousand tokens — versus reading many whole files, because only ~3 targeted bash commands run.

nodes.label: Non-destructive graph generation
nodes.rationale: Graphify does not change any files in the working directory; it only creates a new knowledge-base layer on top, kept in its own separate folder.

nodes.label: ADR (Any Decision Record)
nodes.rationale: Historically 'Architecture Decision Record', renamed 'Any Decision Record' since the arrival of agents because it records not only architecture but every decision about what you work on. Code documents the present ('what is'); the ADR documents the 'why' — why this architecture, which options were rejected, what was tried. Underrated but powerful and easy to set up.

nodes.label: PRD (Product Requirements Document)
nodes.rationale: Captures the 'what': the need, specifications, users, scope. Lives during the discovery phase and is decomposed into user stories / deliverable slices. PRD = the what, ADR = the why; complementary.

nodes.label: MADR (Markdown ADR)
nodes.rationale: Markdown formalism/format for ADRs with many published example templates; a longer variant can specify a parent for navigation, consequences, confirmation, and links to docs.

nodes.label: ADR structure/template
nodes.rationale: A typical ADR structure: title + context, status, decision, considered/rejected options, consequences. Example: choosing JUnit5 for advanced tests records context, considered options, and the decision so other test frameworks are eliminated and never revisited.

nodes.label: Capturing decisions and context
nodes.rationale: Beyond content, the two most important things to store are decisions and context — why a choice was made, why options were rejected. Without written decisions these become invisible and get lost over months, causing bad technical choices that were already rejected to be re-adopted.

nodes.label: Session-restart decision amnesia
nodes.rationale: Every new session the agent restarts from zero and forgets all decisions because the context window is finite — you cannot use one infinite session. A fresh agent (even switching from Claude to Codex) re-proposes options already discarded, forcing the developer to re-correct it each time. Written ADRs let a well-configured agent check them and not re-propose rejected approaches.

nodes.label: Code-vs-decisions iceberg
nodes.rationale: Code is treated as the source of truth for what was decided, but it is only the visible tip of the iceberg. Below it lie many invisible decisions — informal, spoken, done-in-passing — that get lost, especially across developers and time. Code documents the present but not the decisions.

nodes.label: Graphify (queryable knowledge graph)
nodes.rationale: Putting the PRD + ADRs into Graphify yields a genuinely queryable 'brain' — a companion tool the speaker covered in a prior video, positioned as the continuation of this ADR workflow.

nodes.label: Queryable brain
nodes.rationale: The end-state goal: decisions and context stored as permanent, requeryable markdown so any relaunched session can retrieve them — a persistent external brain for the project/business rather than context trapped in an ephemeral session window.

nodes.label: Permanent requeryable rejected options
nodes.rationale: The ADR records which options were rejected and why, kept permanent and requeryable. A well-configured agent checks the ADRs on each session and refuses to re-propose an approach already ruled out — e.g. all other test frameworks are eliminated once JUnit5 is decided.

nodes.label: User story
nodes.rationale: The PRD's need is decomposed into user stories (the application's features). During the project ADRs 'fall out' as decisions change features, remove things, or pick libraries; agents can then reshape/regenerate the stories from the ADRs.

nodes.label: Iterative research-plan-execute-review loop
nodes.rationale: Development is not linear PRD→UserStory→Arch→Code→ADR; ADRs stack up and feed back in an agile iterative loop. The speaker's pipeline: research, plan, redefine user stories, replan, re-execute, re-review, re-deliver. From the ADRs you ask agents to reshape/regenerate the stories, which are re-decomposed, re-tested, and re-dispatched.

nodes.label: Agile/Scrum change-management analogy
nodes.rationale: By analogy with project management, methodologies like Agile and Scrum arose to manage constant change; the classic flow writes the need but almost never the decisions, so decision management (ADRs) fills that gap.

nodes.label: BMAD / SpecKit methods
nodes.rationale: Cited as the major classic methods that go need→specs→architecture→execution (the linear PRD-first flow the ADR loop augments).

nodes.label: ADR kit / Claude Code skill
nodes.rationale: The speaker offers an 'ADR kit' skill: install it and the ADR format is already present, so you capture context and apply it in your projects — a packaged skill to stand up the ADR workflow easily.

nodes.label: ChipSAS boilerplate stack
nodes.rationale: An agent-ready SaaS boilerplate where stack decisions are pre-settled in ADRs: authentication, Stripe subscriptions, admin panel, multitenant, clean architecture, workflow ingest, CI/CD, ORM, emails. In a new project the architecture phase is already resolved by the boilerplate, so you just apply the story to it.

nodes.label: Obsidian markdown vault
nodes.rationale: Everything — content, decisions, context — is stored as markdown in a vault like Obsidian; the speaker keeps business-level ADRs in his Obsidian vault, with project-level ADRs living inside each project.

nodes.label: Multi-level ADRs
nodes.rationale: ADRs apply at several levels: general business/activity level, per-project or per-project-cycle level, and even personal life (e.g. a gym/nutrition protocol). Even Google Cloud uses an ADR system, showing how widespread the practice is.

nodes.label: Fable 5
nodes.rationale: Presented as an exceptionally powerful frontier model the speaker spent thousands in credits testing; temporarily available and expected to return to subscriptions per Anthropic.

nodes.label: Opus 4.8
nodes.rationale: Cheaper model the speaker wants to elevate toward Fable-5-like behavior by injecting Fable's process; Opus 4.8 on high is often as good as or better than max-effort runs.

nodes.label: The process, not the model, is the moat
nodes.rationale: A beginner with Fable 5 loses to Karpathy on an older model; the way you instruct and the loops/systems you build around a model matter more than raw intelligence. You can't keep a model's intelligence but you can keep its process.

nodes.label: Extract the Fable method
nodes.rationale: Rather than have Fable plan and do everything, extract how Fable thinks and let cheaper models think and execute like it; analyze a loved deliverable (or the session) to recover what made it good and turn it into a reusable skill.

nodes.label: Treat the top model as teacher, not workhorse
nodes.rationale: Use the powerful model like a retiring senior engineer packaging its knowledge for junior engineers — improving your setups and skills — rather than as an employee grinding tasks.

nodes.label: Fable mode skill
nodes.rationale: An installable skill file that makes any model (Opus 4.8, GPT 5.5, open-source) operate with Fable's judgment, planning, verification and reasoning habits; makes Opus feel elevated.

nodes.label: Five gates: scope, evidence, attack, verify, report
nodes.rationale: Fable mode operates on five gates: scoping before work, evidence before reasoning, reasoning adversarially, verifying before declaring done, and calibrating/reporting.

nodes.label: Adversarial scoping (devil's advocate planning)
nodes.rationale: Beyond listing steps, scoping means playing devil's advocate — enumerating everything that could go wrong and exploring the unknowns in the plan; Fable excels at this.

nodes.label: Verify existence; recognition is not current knowledge
nodes.rationale: From the leaked prompt: partial recognition from training is not current knowledge, and a prompt implying a file exists doesn't mean it does — verify at every step that what was done is accurate.

nodes.label: Leaked Claude Fable 5 system prompt
nodes.rationale: Leaked system prompt the speaker read (with Fable) to extract habits: verify existence, answer an ambiguous query before asking one clarifying question, acknowledge errors, and effort-scaling guidance.

nodes.label: Dynamic workflows
nodes.rationale: Claude Code lets you spin up dynamic workflows where a model designs the workflow and its sub-agents; the orchestrator keeps designing more steps as workers report back.

nodes.label: Orchestrator-to-subagent delegation
nodes.rationale: An orchestrator model designs and plans while sub-agents (Sonnet/high-q/Opus) execute and report back, letting the orchestrator plan further steps in a loop.

nodes.label: Fable orchestrating Sonnet workers ≈ Fable+Fable results
nodes.rationale: Dynamic workflows with Fable orchestrating Sonnet sub-agents gave about the same results as Fable orchestrating Fable, at far lower cost — the light-bulb moment: keep the orchestrator's planning, cheapen the execution.

nodes.label: Opus orchestrator + high-q scouts (~3x cheaper, same result)
nodes.rationale: Concrete test: Opus orchestrator (in Fable mode) delegating to high-q scout workers was ~3x cheaper than using Opus/Sonnet workers yet produced the exact same result.

nodes.label: Model routing
nodes.rationale: Match each task to the smallest/cheapest model that can hit the required quality — don't pay for a high-intelligence model when a cheaper one gets the same result; a key skill for coming years.

nodes.label: Reasoning effort levels (low/high/xhigh/max)
nodes.rationale: Beyond choosing a model, choose the effort level per task: 1 for signal facts, 3-5 for medium tasks, 5-10 for deep research; Fable 5 on low ≈ Opus 4.8 on high.

nodes.label: Higher effort can overthink and produce worse output
nodes.rationale: xhigh/max effort runs go longer, cost more, and second-guess themselves — often producing something worse than Opus 4.8 or Fable on plain high.

nodes.label: Score-vs-cost-vs-effort release-blog chart
nodes.rationale: Release-blog chart plotting Fable 5, Opus 4.8, and GPT 5.5 by score (y) vs cost (x) across effort levels, showing overlap like Fable-5-low near Opus-4.8-high.

nodes.label: Model toolkit table (cost/intelligence/taste)
nodes.rationale: Give Claude a table of available models scored on cost, intelligence, and taste (creativity/UX) so it can route/delegate to the right model when spinning up dynamic workflows and agent teams.

nodes.label: Delegate to Codex / open-source models
nodes.rationale: Routing can hand tasks to external tools like Codex or open-source/local models, not just Anthropic models.

nodes.label: AI unit economics / per-month budget
nodes.rationale: As companies and individuals face AI budgets, routing to the cheapest sufficient model is what separates people getting far more for far less.

nodes.label: Long-task loop: plan, execute, verify
nodes.rationale: Anthropic published that Fable is strong at long tasks: working toward a goal, planning it, executing, then verifying.

nodes.label: You don't own the models — own your process
nodes.rationale: Fable will be pulled and returned to subscriptions; models can be taken away, so what you can own is your processes, systems, methodologies, plus hardware and local models.

nodes.label: Claude Code
nodes.rationale: The harness that lets the speaker spin up dynamic workflows, sub-agents, and skills.

nodes.label: Second Brain knowledge system
nodes.rationale: A trustworthy personal-knowledge system built on ~2000 markdown notes; the goal was not a pretty graph but a system you can trust to make knowledge findable and self-consistent.

nodes.label: Four required capabilities (find, read, stay-clean, overview)
nodes.rationale: Define CAPABILITIES before tools: (1) semantic find in seconds without the exact word, (2) read in-place, (3) stay clean (detect duplicates/contradictions/stale), (4) overview. Each capability later finds a home in the architecture.

nodes.label: Six-step build process (4 steps before any code)
nodes.rationale: Question -> capabilities -> brainstorm -> spec -> plan -> build -> test. Four of six steps happen before the first line of code; the biggest AI-project risk is the AI confidently building the wrong thing at full speed, and front-loading decisions prevents it.

nodes.label: Superpowers skill package for Claude Code
nodes.rationale: Skill package supplying steps 2-5; a skill is a work instruction given to Claude once that then applies to every project. It forces the AI into an engineering process: ask questions one at a time, write decisions to a doc, break into small testable tasks, then build, with the human as a checkpoint at each transition.

nodes.label: Fresh subagent per task with isolated context
nodes.rationale: In the build step each task gets a fresh Claude subagent (own instance) that receives only that one task plus the context it needs and nothing else; its result is verified before the next task starts.

nodes.label: Per-task test + explicit done-criterion
nodes.rationale: Each of the 14 core tasks carries a test and a concrete completion criterion (e.g. 'search returns THIS file for THIS example question', not merely 'search works'); the human reviews results, not code.

nodes.label: Deterministic code before the model
nodes.rationale: AI (purple in the diagram) is used only where genuine understanding is needed; anything a machine can do reliably runs as plain deterministic code because it is faster, cheaper, reproducible and auditable.

nodes.label: Markdown as the single source of truth
nodes.rationale: All knowledge lives as plain markdown files; every other part (index, graph, search) is a derived view that can be discarded and rebuilt without losing any knowledge. No database, no lock-in format.

nodes.label: Deterministic indexer (map + index.md catalog)
nodes.rationale: A small no-AI program that walks all folders and records title/size, links and cluster per file, then writes a map file (for the graph) and an index.md catalog (one line per area/important file) that Claude reads first. Every run is identical, takes seconds and costs nothing.

nodes.label: QMD local hybrid search (Tobi Lutke)
nodes.rationale: A ready-made 'building block' reference: local hybrid search combining classic keyword search with meaning search, runs fully on-device with three small local models (~<3GB), no cloud. Answers capability #1 (find without exact word).

nodes.label: Semantic/meaning (embedding) search
nodes.rationale: A small model places every note on a 'map of meanings'; the query is embedded onto the same map and nearest neighbours are the hits, so 'subtitles' finds a note that only says 'captions' with no word overlap.

nodes.label: Brain First rule set in CLAUDE.md
nodes.rationale: The rules Claude reads at every start that govern how it works with the knowledge; its heart is the search ladder, forbidding Claude from wildly searching or reading the whole vault.

nodes.label: Five-rung search ladder
nodes.rationale: Rung1 read index.md catalog; rung2 check the wiki for already-condensed knowledge; rung3 QMD search; rung4 open exactly one (best) file; rung5 answer. Descending the ladder keeps token use to a few thousand per question.

nodes.label: System that needs no large context window
nodes.rationale: You never load the whole vault into context; the trick is not a bigger context window but a system that needs none — Claude loads only the catalog plus a few relevant files per question. The context window is the AI's short-term memory and more tokens means fuller, slower, costlier.

nodes.label: Karpathy pattern: AI maintains its own knowledge wiki
nodes.rationale: A 'pattern to adapt' reference (a few paragraphs, not code) from Andrej Karpathy: the AI writes and updates markdown pages, keeps a small index read first, and runs check-rules against contradictions and dead links. Answers capability #3 (stay clean).

nodes.label: Self-maintaining wiki (0.9 Wiki folder)
nodes.rationale: A folder written exclusively by Claude, never by hand; page types include source summaries, topic pages, person/project pages and synthesis pages that condense multiple sources; all cross-linked, same rules regardless of which Claude session works on it.

nodes.label: Wiki schema file (Claude's wiki work-instruction)
nodes.rationale: A plain text file living in the wiki folder that Claude reads before every wiki operation, defining page types and rules so behaviour stays identical across sessions.

nodes.label: Ingest operation (add a source into the wiki)
nodes.rationale: Claude reads a new source, uses the wiki page-directory to find only the pages the topic touches (not the whole wiki), updates/creates those pages and sets links; matching info is worked in, contradicting info is flagged.

nodes.label: Contradiction detection & flagging (52 real conflicts)
nodes.rationale: During ingest every touched page is checked for whether new info contradicts existing content; conflicts are boxed wherever they appear and nothing is overwritten — both statements stay with their sources and the human decides. On the real vault it flagged 52 conflict spots (a planted-trap test caught all 3 and left an unrelated 4th untouched).

nodes.label: Three reference roles (building block / pattern / benchmark)
nodes.rationale: Every reference gets one explicit role: Baustein (ready-made part to install), Muster (an idea/pattern you re-implement), Messlatte (a benchmark defining how good the result must look). Copy-pasting others' solutions solves others' problems; roles are the most transferable idea in the project.

nodes.label: Graphify & G-Brain (pattern-only references)
nodes.rationale: Two projects studied for their pattern (markdown as sole truth, graph only a derived intermediate) with no code taken; knowing what you do NOT need also gives confidence to stick to your own approach — rejecting is a research result too.

nodes.label: Human decides, AI delivers
nodes.rationale: Across brainstorming, every wiki contradiction and every optics step the AI made not a single decision — it only made decisions possible. The AI writes the code; you curate references, decide, and sign off each step.

nodes.label: Benchmark: ~50% fewer tokens, ~40% less time
nodes.rationale: Five real work questions run with and without the Brain system: the Brain variant used ~50% fewer tokens and ~40% less time at 5/5 correct. Token cost comes from searching (each search step re-reads the whole prior history); without a system Claude searches long and each round gets costlier — one query cost >0.5M tokens without Brain vs ~a third with it. Brain wins on deeply-buried knowledge spanning multiple files.

nodes.label: Images as spec (ChatGPT generates target, Claude Code builds)
nodes.rationale: The expensive mistake was describing the desired look in words (a guessing game). The fix: have ChatGPT's image engine generate the target-state images, then hand them to Claude Code as a concrete visual reference/spec — a clear division of labour between two AIs, one step at a time with human sign-off.

nodes.label: Graph web app (read-only viewer window)
nodes.rationale: The fifth building block, deliberately at the edge: it reads the indexer's map file to draw the graph, opens any clicked note beside it (capability #2 read), and hosts the semantic search box. It never writes and never decides — the optics are swappable; the foundation is mandatory.

nodes.label: AI second brain as persistent knowledge graph
nodes.rationale: Coding assistants have amnesia: every new chat re-reads files from scratch, paying a token tax per query, and stuffing a whole repo into context makes answers worse. Do the expensive reading once, up front, into a graph the assistant can query.

nodes.label: read-once, then walk the graph
nodes.rationale: Instead of searching text on every question, do the expensive reading once up front and squeeze the project into an explicit graph of entities and relationships; answering then means walking the graph. Exactly how a senior engineer works: build the mental map once, then follow it.

nodes.label: RAG vector search is weak for code
nodes.rationale: RAG chunks files into vectors and finds closest matches, but for code the link between a function and its callers is not about similar words — it lives in the call graph. You pay to load the whole library just to find one paragraph.

nodes.label: three-pass ingestion
nodes.rationale: graphify runs three separate passes, each with a different privacy story: source code, recordings, and documents/images.

nodes.label: pass one: Tree-sitter source parsing
nodes.rationale: Source code runs through Tree-sitter across 40 languages — deterministic, no AI model, no network call. Code stays on-machine and every link found is tagged as a hard fact ('extracted').

nodes.label: pass two: local transcription with faster-whisper
nodes.rationale: Audio/video (e.g. a screen-recorded design meeting) is transcribed locally with faster-whisper — an hour of talking becomes text on the laptop, nothing uploaded, folded into the graph.

nodes.label: pass three: documents/images sent to AI provider
nodes.rationale: PDFs and images can't be read by a grammar, so they are sent to your own AI provider (your own API key, no middleman) to be understood. The only pass that leaves your machine; skip it with code-only mode for full privacy.

nodes.label: code-only (fully private) mode
nodes.rationale: Run code-only mode to skip the document/image pass so nothing leaves the machine.

nodes.label: confidence labels: extracted/inferred/ambiguous
nodes.rationale: Every connection carries a label so the tool refuses to sound sure about what it isn't: Extracted = right there in the code, trust it; Inferred = model reasoned from context, probably; Ambiguous = model raised a hand, check before building on it.

nodes.label: community detection into modules
nodes.rationale: Once the graph exists it self-organizes: community detection makes modules fall out of the shape of the code (auth, billing, infrastructure clusters) with bridge files lit up — you didn't draw the map, the relationships did.

nodes.label: query-time graph walk returning short paths
nodes.rationale: Asking 'how does a user log in?' walks the graph and hands back a short path — three hops, zero files opened — so the assistant reads a few node summaries instead of half the repo.

nodes.label: explain / path / deep query modes
nodes.rationale: graphify explain gives a plain-English tour of an entity; graphify path finds the shortest chain between two things; deep mode hunts the fuzzier inferred links a quick pass would skip.

nodes.label: ~71x fewer tokens per query (unreproduced)
nodes.rationale: graphify claims about 71x fewer tokens per query on a mixed codebase — but it is their own benchmark on their own data, unreproduced externally, so take the exact figure with a grain of salt though the direction is right.

nodes.label: graph exports: HTML/JSON/markdown/Neo4j/MCP
nodes.rationale: The graph isn't trapped in the tool: exports include a clickable viz.js HTML graph, plain JSON, a markdown report of busiest files, a Neo4j database, and an MCP server so any agent can call the codebase graph as a built-in tool.

nodes.label: MCP server export
nodes.rationale: An MCP server export lets any agent call your codebase graph like a built-in tool.

nodes.label: incremental SHA-256 hashing + git hook rebuild
nodes.rationale: Every file gets a SHA-256 hash so a second run only rereads what changed; add a git hook and the graph rebuilds on every commit, keeping the map matched to the exact branch you're standing on.

nodes.label: Obsidian export (entities as pages, wikilinks)
nodes.rationale: Obsidian is plain markdown on your disk — the natural home for a brain you own. graphify's Obsidian export turns each entity into a page and each relationship into a wikilink, and the graph view shows the codebase as a mind map you can wander.

nodes.label: Claudian: Claude Code in the Obsidian sidebar
nodes.rationale: Claudian drops Claude Code into the Obsidian sidebar so the vault becomes the agent's working directory — it can read notes, write new ones, and run commands without leaving the app.

nodes.label: Karpathy three-layer design (raw/wiki/schema)
nodes.rationale: Karpathy's design is three layers: a raw folder of sources the AI leaves untouched; a wiki layer the AI owns and keeps cross-links honest; and a schema file telling the model how the wiki is laid out. Kept alive by three operations: ingest, query, lint.

nodes.label: ingest / query / lint operations
nodes.rationale: Ingest pulls in new sources and updates every page they touch; query searches the wiki and answers with citations; lint runs a health check for contradictions, dead pages, and stale claims.

nodes.label: lint / weekly maintenance pass
nodes.rationale: The biggest crack: Karpathy built a cleanup (lint) pass into the design on purpose and almost every tutorial skips it. Five minutes a week reading what the agent wrote, pruning stale pages and killing contradictions, is the rent — skip it and the whole thing decays into noise.

nodes.label: Memex (Vannevar Bush, 1945) lineage
nodes.rationale: Karpathy traces the idea to Vannevar Bush's 1945 Memex — the dream of linked trails between documents where the links matter as much as the pages. We just lacked a tool patient enough to maintain them; now the model does that upkeep for free.

nodes.label: crack: scale degrades navigation
nodes.rationale: A vault of 50 tidy notes feels like magic; 2,000 notes becomes a search problem again because the model spends its context navigating structure instead of answering. The graph softens this but doesn't erase it.

nodes.label: crack: confident fiction (fabricated notes)
nodes.rationale: When writing up notes the assistant fills gaps with things you never said, in your own voice. The fix is a blunt instruction: 'do not add anything I didn't write' — without it, the second brain invents memories.

nodes.label: crack: feedback loops (AI quoting AI)
nodes.rationale: The AI writes a page, later cites its own page as a source, then builds on it. Left unattended you get AI quoting AI, where small mistakes slowly harden into facts — the brain fills with its own echo.
